Name: kernel_harmonization
Title: Kernel Harmonization: A New Approach to Optimize Inference Latency via Hardware-Driven Fusion of Deep Learning Kernels
Short Hypothesis: We hypothesize that leveraging hardware-specific profiling to harmonize and fuse multiple deep learning kernels can significantly reduce inference latency and improve throughput on commodity GPUs, outperforming existing techniques.
Related Work: Existing works primarily focus on single kernel optimizations or high-level abstractions that do not consider hardware-specific tuning during kernel fusion. Our approach distinguishes itself by prioritizing empirical performance data from real hardware, advocating for a profiling-first methodology to achieve optimal kernel harmonization, therefore addressing current limitations of existing solutions that do not fully exploit the hardware capabilities during inference.
Abstract: This proposal introduces a novel approach termed Kernel Harmonization, which aims to optimize inference latency in deep learning models through hardware-driven fusion of kernels. Unlike existing methods that apply generalized optimizations, we will implement a profiling-first strategy, meticulously measuring kernel performance on commodity GPUs using tools like NVProf and Nsight. By dynamically fusing multiple kernels based on detailed hardware profiling data, we seek to minimize memory access overhead and improve the overall computational efficiency of inference tasks. Our approach will involve a systematic evaluation of the effects of different fusion strategies on latency and throughput, employing a series of A/B tests across various deep learning architectures. The goal is to establish a clear correlation between kernel harmonization techniques and tangible improvements in real-world inference scenarios. The results will be validated through rigorous benchmarking, focusing on latency metrics such as p50/p99 and throughput under varying batch sizes, ultimately providing practitioners with actionable insights for deploying optimized models.
Experiments: 1. Implement kernel harmonization techniques on a set of popular deep learning models (e.g., ResNet, BERT) using Triton and CUDA. 2. Profile individual kernels using NVProf to gather baseline metrics (latency, throughput, memory bandwidth). 3. Design and implement dynamic kernel fusion strategies based on profiling data to reduce memory access overhead. 4. Conduct A/B latency races comparing original and harmonized kernels under identical conditions. 5. Measure and report p50 and p99 latency, throughput under different batch sizes, and energy consumption per inference. 6. Validate results across multiple GPU architectures to ensure robustness and generalizability.
Risk Factors and Limitations: Potential risks include the possibility of diminishing returns with kernel fusion, leading to increased complexity without significant performance gains. There may also be limitations in scalability on larger models or more complex architectures. Additionally, the reliance on specific hardware may limit the applicability of findings across diverse GPU setups.
